topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. A profile picture loaded: a familiar, sneering grin I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. It was Mark. The boy who used to orchestrate elaborate humiliations in the schoolyard. A wave of nausea washed over me, a familiar, cold tightening in my stomach. I stared at the request, my thumb hovering over the 'Accept' button as if it were a live wire. Every muscle in my jaw clenched. Why now? What did he want? I quickly minimized the message, the screen suddenly too bright, and retreated to the dim light of my room, pulling the blanket up to my chin, trying to make myself small and invisible. The silence of my apartment felt heavy, pressing in on me.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm, and the sight of the name made my hands tremble slightly. It was him. The memory of his sharp words and the way he’d always found the precise angle to make me feel utterly exposed flooded my mind. A knot formed deep in my throat, making it difficult to swallow. I reread the request three times, the familiar urge to flee warring with a strange, reluctant curiosity. My gaze drifted to the window, watching the streetlights blur through the damp air. I felt a profound inertia; the usual momentum that propelled me forward had simply ceased. I let the phone drop onto the bedspread. The weight of the unread message seemed to anchor me to the spot, a dull, persistent ache behind my ribs that refused to dissipate.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, pulling me from a deep concentration on a textbook. My heart began a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. It was a connection request, and the sender's name sent a jolt of icy dread through me. I didn't move for a long moment, just sitting bolt upright, my fingers gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. The world seemed to narrow down to the glowing rectangle in my hand. A strange mix of apprehension and a paralyzing sense of obligation settled over me. I looked around my room, at the familiar posters and cluttered shelves, and felt utterly unable to shift my focus from this incoming demand. I slowly, deliberately, tapped the notification, the sound of the confirmation echoing too loudly in the sudden stillness.
